title: Emergency: Dog Eyeball Bulging Out of Socket (Proptosis)
meta_desc: If your dog's eye is bulging, this is a high-urgency medical emergency. Learn about proptosis and why you must see a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
What is Proptosis?

Proptosis is a serious medical condition where the eyeball is displaced forward, protruding out of the bony eye socket. This usually results in the eyelids being unable to close, leaving the delicate surface of the eye exposed to the air. It is most commonly caused by trauma, such as a fight with another animal, a fall, or an accident.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-urgency medical emergency. Time is of the essence; the longer the eye remains out of the socket, the higher the risk of permanent blindness, nerve damage, or loss of the eye itself. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and seek care without delay.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

In an emergency situation, providing a clear photo to your veterinarian or triage professional can be lifesaving. A visual assessment allows the medical team to determine the severity of the displacement, check for bleeding or rupture, and prepare the necessary surgical equipment or medications before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and try to keep the eye moist using sterile saline solution or a clean, damp cloth. Do not attempt to push the eye back into the socket yourself.

faqs.question: Can the eye be saved?
faqs.answer: Whether the eye can be saved depends on the severity of the injury, the breed of the dog, and how quickly they receive treatment. In many cases, surgery is required to reposition the eye.

faqs.question: Is this more common in certain breeds?
faqs.answer: Yes, brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Pugs, Bulldogs, and Shih Tzus are more prone to proptosis because their eye sockets are naturally shallow.
